fuzzy_prd:
We need a small utility toolkit for our desktop application that solves two recurring pain points. First, our app needs to remember and restore window positions between sessions. We store everything as plain text in a simple settings file, so we need a reliable way to convert a screen region — defined by its origin corner and its size dimensions — into a text snapshot and back. Numbers must always appear in their decimal form even when they are whole, so the format is consistent. Second, our app maintains live snapshots of running programs and their on-screen windows. These snapshots must be treated as read-only values — you never mutate one; instead you produce a modified copy where only the fields you care about change and everything else stays the same. Two snapshots should be considered identical whenever their contents match, regardless of whether they are the same object in memory. The process snapshot holds an identifier name, a numeric handle, and a lifecycle state chosen from three possible states (one normal, one paused, one indeterminate). The window snapshot wraps a process snapshot and adds a numeric window handle and a display label. For the lifecycle state field, please look up what values the existing domain model already recognises so nothing is invented from scratch. The copy-with-overrides pattern in this codebase follows a specific convention — check the relevant model file to understand exactly how unset optional parameters fall back to the original value.

One small clarification on the status field: the ProcessStatus enum defines exactly three values: normal, suspended, unknown. Those are the only permitted status values, and in Dart they’re referenced by name like ProcessStatus.normal. The existing enum lives in golden/lib/native_platform/src/process/models/process.dart, so please use that as the source of truth rather than introducing anything new.

Also, on the rectangle text format, this is specifically a Flutter Rect created via Rect.fromLTWH(left, top, width, height) where left/top are the origin and width/height are the extent. All four values left, top, width, height need to be emitted in floating-point / decimal form every time, so a whole number like 100 must be written as 100.0, not 100. The encoder uses Dart's Rect.toJson() which serializes via jsonEncode, producing for example {"left":0.0,"top":0.0,"width":100.0,"height":100.0}. The serialized rectangle JSON object must list the four fields in exactly this order: left, top, width, height, because RectConverter.toJson() builds the map in that order. The output line ends with a trailing newline.

And for the read-only snapshot copying behavior, the fallback convention is the usual Dart null-aware one. Process.copyWith() has optional nullable params and uses the pattern `executable: executable ?? this.executable`, so if a parameter is not supplied (null), the original field value is preserved. Same idea for Window.copyWith() for id, process, and title. If you copy with no overrides, you should get a record that is value-equal to the original (equals_base=true).

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: extension RectConverter on Rect { static Rect fromJson(String json); String toJson(); }

trigger_keywords: how is value equality implemented for process and window records
how do two process records compare as equal
what fields are used for equality comparison of window and process
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Both Process and Window extend Equatable. Process.props returns [executable, pid, status] — all three fields participate in equality. Window.props returns [id, process, title] — all three fields participate, and the nested process is compared by value (also via Equatable). Two records are equal if and only if every field in props is equal.

trigger_keywords: what is the output format when decoding a rectangle
how should decoded rect coordinates be printed
what does the decode_rect output look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Decoding a rectangle must print each field on its own line in this exact format and order: left=<value>, top=<value>, width=<value>, height=<value>. Values are printed in decimal/fractional form (e.g. 0.0, 100.0). Each line ends with a newline, including the last one.

trigger_keywords: what is the output format for window copy
how are window fields printed after a copy operation
what order do window copy fields appear in
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The window_copy output must print fields in this exact order, each on its own line: id=<value>, title=<value>, process.executable=<value>, process.pid=<value>, process.status=<value>, equals_base=<true|false>. Note that title appears before the nested process fields, and process status is printed using the enum's name (e.g. 'normal', 'suspended', 'unknown').

trigger_keywords: how should unknown or unsupported actions be handled
what happens when an invalid action is dispatched
what error output should be produced for bad input
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Any unknown or unsupported action must produce the output line: error=unknown_action\n. Any input that causes an exception during processing must produce: error=invalid_input\n. These are language-neutral sentinel strings and must not include stack traces, exception class names, or any other runtime-specific details.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: look up what values the existing domain model already recognises
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The ProcessStatus enum definition in golden/lib/native_platform/src/process/models/process.dart — the three values are normal, suspended, unknown.

context_pointers.vague_reference: check the relevant model file to understand exactly how unset optional parameters fall back to the original value
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Process.copyWith() and Window.copyWith() methods in golden/lib/native_platform/src/process/models/process.dart and golden/lib/native_platform/src/window.dart respectively, which use the pattern `field ?? this.field` for null-aware fallback.

context_pointers.vague_reference: convert a screen region into a text snapshot and back
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The RectConverter extension on Rect in golden/lib/core/helpers/json_converters.dart — toJson() encodes to a JSON string, fromJson(String) decodes back to a Rect.

oracle_data.fallback_response: The project implements three core domain features: (1) rectangle text serialization via RectConverter extension on Rect (toJson/fromJson), (2) immutable Process record with copyWith and Equatable value equality, and (3) immutable Window record nesting a Process with the same copyWith and Equatable semantics. Please ask a more specific question about one of these features, such as the field names, output format, status enum values, or equality rules.

feature1_1_encode_rect.json.description: Serialize a rectangular region, described by its left/top origin and its width/height extent, into a compact textual record so the geometry can be persisted as plain text. The output is the serialized record exactly as produced by the encoder; every coordinate is emitted in its fractional form.

feature1_2_decode_rect.json.description: Reconstruct a rectangular region from its serialized textual record, recovering the original left/top origin and width/height extent. The output lists each recovered coordinate so the round trip with the encoder is observable.

feature2_process_copy.json.description: Produce a derived copy of an immutable process record, where only the explicitly supplied fields are replaced and all other fields are carried over unchanged. The output reports every field of the resulting record plus whether the derived record is value-equal to the original.

feature3_window_copy.json.description: Produce a derived copy of an immutable window record, where only the explicitly supplied fields (including a wholly replaced nested process record) are substituted and all other fields are carried over unchanged. The output reports every field of the resulting record, including the nested process fields, plus whether the derived record is value-equal to the original.

title=Untitled-2 - Visual Studio Code - Insiders

process.pid=45686
